coredata.dc:description: Rheumatoid arthritis is a chronic autoimmune disorder characterized by the pathological accumulation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which contributes to sustained inflammation and tissue damage. Current therapeutic modalities frequently exhibit low rates of clinical remission, and effectively mitigating the oxidative stress microenvironment associated with excessive ROS presents a significant challenge. Herein, we present a novel carbon dots (CDs) with multi-enzyme activity, featuring a porphyrin iron core and surface functional groups rich in phosphorus and oxygen, providing abundant catalytic sites for enhanced scavenging of hydrogen peroxide, hydroxyl radicals, and superoxide anions. Furthermore, the phosphate groups on the surface of CDs exhibited a specific affinity for hydroxyapatite, thereby facilitating targeted delivery to the bone. The localized accumulation of CDs with multi-enzyme activity at inflammatory lesions can effectively reduce oxidative stress levels, subsequently inhibiting bone destruction in collagen-induced arthritis rat models and significantly alleviating disease symptoms. This study integrates multiple catalytic sites and targeting moieties into a single nanostructure, representing a promising strategy for the precise treatment of oxidative stress-related bone pathologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characteristics of CDs. HRTEM and size distribution histogram of (a) CDs1, (b) CDs2, and (c) CDs3, scale bar = 20 nm; (d) Zeta potential, (e) FTIR, and (f) XPS spectra of CDs1, CDs2, and CDs3; High-resolution (g) C1s, (h) O1s, and (i) P2p spectra of CDs3.

Activity and mechanism of CDs in scavenging free radicals. ESR spectra of CDs scavenging (a) •OH and (b) O2 •-; (c) Bubble generation images upon the addition of CDs1, CDs2, and CDs3 to H2O2; (d) Schematic diagram of the configuration changes during CDs3-catalyzed H2O2 reaction; Step diagrams of adsorption energy changes of CDs1, CDs2, and CDs3 in catalyzing (e) H2O2, (f) •OH, and (g) O2 •-.

In vitro antioxidant activity of CDs. (a) Hemolysis percentage. Inset: centrifugation results of each sample group, NC: PBS, PC: 0.1 % TritonX-100; (b) Laser confocal images after co-incubation of CDs1 and CDs3 with RAW264.7, scale bar = 10 μm; (c) Representative fluorescent images of intracellular ROS staining under different treatment conditions, scale bar = 30 μm.

Bone-tissue targeting capability of CDs. (a) Calculations of binding energies of three different structural CDs with HA; (b) Fluorescence images demonstrating the binding capability of CDs at different concentrations with HA; (c) Binding energies calculations of three different structural CDs with HA, all data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3).

Bone-tissue targeting capability of CDs. (a) Biodistribution of CDs1 and CDs3 at different time points in CIA rats; (b) Statistical analysis of fluorescence intensity accumulation in the limbs at various time points, data are shown as means ± SD of biological duplicates, statistical significance was calculated using two-way ANOVA followed by Bonferroni's multiple comparisons test, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗p < 0.05; (c) Representative fluorescence images of bone fragments after incubation with CDs1-Cy5/CDs3-Cy5 and (d) their corresponding average radiant efficiencies, data are expressed as mean ± SD (n = 3), an unpaired student's t-test was performed for intergroup comparisons. A two-tailed test was utilized to evaluate the directionality of differences, ∗∗p < 0.01; (e) Ex vivo fluorescence images of limbs at 8 h post-injection of CDs1 and CDs3, and (f) fluorescence intensity statistics, data are expressed as mean ± SD (n = 3). An unpaired student's t-test was performed for intergroup comparisons. A two-tailed test was utilized to evaluate the directionality of differences, ∗∗∗p < 0.001.

In vivo efficacy assessment of CDs. (a) Body weight; (b) AI scores; (c) Statistical changes in ankle joint thickness, data are shown as mean ± SD of biological duplicates, statistical significance was calculated using two-way ANOVA followed by Tukey's multiple comparisons test, ∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01; (d) Pathological H&E staining results of ankle joints, scale bar = 100 μm; (e) Representative micro-CT images of joints.

Schematic diagram of the structural variation of CDs with increasing molar ratio of alendronate and their mechanisms for targeting bone tissue and enhancing ROS clearance. Three types of CDs with different phosphate groups contents were synthesized by adjusting alendronate sodium. These include CDs1 (no phosphate groups), CDs2 (low phosphate content), and CDs3 (high phosphate content). The abundant phosphate groups on CDs3 enable specific binding to Ca2+ in bone tissues, facilitating bone targeting. Additionally, phosphorus doping enhances the ROS scavenging ability of CDs3.

head.ce:title: Enhanced multi-enzyme activity and bone-targeted phosphorus-doped carbon dots for precise antioxidant therapy of rheumatoid arthritis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rheumatoid arthritis is a chronic autoimmune disorder characterized by the pathological accumulation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which contributes to sustained inflammation and tissue damage. Current therapeutic modalities frequently exhibit low rates of clinical remission, and effectively mitigating the oxidative stress microenvironment associated with excessive ROS presents a significant challenge. Herein, we present a novel carbon dots (CDs) with multi-enzyme activity, featuring a porphyrin iron core and surface functional groups rich in phosphorus and oxygen, providing abundant catalytic sites for enhanced scavenging of hydrogen peroxide, hydroxyl radicals, and superoxide anions. Furthermore, the phosphate groups on the surface of CDs exhibited a specific affinity for hydroxyapatite, thereby facilitating targeted delivery to the bone. The localized accumulation of CDs with multi-enzyme activity at inflammatory lesions can effectively reduce oxidative stress levels, subsequently inhibiting bone destruction in collagen-induced arthritis rat models and significantly alleviating disease symptoms. This study integrates multiple catalytic sites and targeting moieties into a single nanostructure, representing a promising strategy for the precise treatment of oxidative stress-related bone pathologies.

ce:section.ce:para: Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is an autoimmune disorder that primarily manifests as chronic, erosive polyarthritis, frequently accompanied by progressive disability, systemic complications, and increased risk of premature mortality [1]. The global prevalence of RA is approximately 0.5 %–1.0 %, affecting individuals of all ages, but is most common between 30 and 50 years, with a prevalence in females 2 to 3 times higher than that in males [2]. RA is associated with various comorbidities, including cardiovascular diseases (such as stroke), scleritis, obesity, bronchial diseases, lipid abnormalities, and joint destruction. The exact etiology of RA remains unclear, but studies have shown that the excessive production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and the formation of a hypoxic inflammatory microenvironment play significant roles in its pathogenesis [3,4]. Abnormal increases in ROS can induce the abnormal differentiation of macrophages and osteoclasts, leading to severe tissue damage and chronic inflammation [5–7]. Modulating ROS levels and ameliorating the hypoxic microenvironment are effective strategies for treating RA.

Current pharmacological interventions for the management of RA include nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) for alleviating pain and inflammation, disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs) such as methotrexate to decelerate disease progression, biological agents such as infliximab and tocilizumab targeting inflammatory mediators, and glucocorticoids such as prednisone for rapid anti-inflammatory effects [8]. While these therapies can decelerate RA progression, they often exhibit significant side effects and off-target effects, resulting in clinical remission rates of less than 50 %. Moreover, they fail to effectively manage the highly oxidative stress microenvironment that exacerbates RA progression. Therefore, the development of a novel formulation with specific and efficient therapeutic effects in the RA microenvironment is crucial.

Nanozymes constitute a category of synthetic enzymes that demonstrate multiple catalytic activities analogous to those of natural enzymes. They possess several advantageous properties, including high stability, excellent biocompatibility, and cost-effectiveness, which have rendered them a subject of significant research interest in biomedical applications pertaining to oxidative stress [9–12]. At present, most nanozymes regulate the levels of ROS, reactive nitrogen species, and specific intracellular biomolecules by mimicking the enzymatic activities of superoxide dismutase, catalase, and glutathione peroxidase, thereby inhibiting disease progression [13–16]. Carbon dots (CDs), a class of photoluminescent nanomaterials, have garnered significant research attention over the past decade due to their unique properties [17–20]. In recent years, CDs have demonstrated catalytic activity owing to their size effects and abundant active sites [21,22]. Through the implementation of diverse control strategies, including size manipulation, morphological alterations, doping processes, vacancy creation, surface modification techniques, and compositional adjustments, researchers have achieved precise regulation of the catalytic activity of CDs [23]. Notably, single-atom nanozymes have exhibited excellent catalytic activity and selectivity in enzyme-like catalysis due to their well-defined structures, designed coordination environments, and optimal atomic utilization efficiency [24]. However, non-specific scavenging of ROS in vivo could result in redox imbalance, highlighting the importance of targeted delivery of CDs to lesion sites for effective antioxidant therapy.

In recent years, the increasing incidence of various skeletal diseases and age-related skeletal abnormalities, including osteoporosis, osteoarthritis, osteomyelitis, and bone cancer, has prompted extensive research into novel bone microenvironment-targeted biomaterials [25–27]. Bisphosphonates (BPs), such as alendronate, are analogues of inorganic pyrophosphate that can strongly chelate calcium ions (Ca2+), demonstrating high affinity and rapid adsorption capabilities on bone surfaces [28]. Inspired by this, we synthesized bone-targeting CDs with multi-enzyme activity using hemin chloride, urea, and alendronate as precursors (Scheme 1 ). With the increase in BP content, the phosphorus content on the surface of the CDs increases, significantly enhancing their bone targeting and ROS scavenging capabilities. These multifunctional CDs selectively eliminate excessive ROS at RA lesion sites, enabling precise antioxidant therapy. This work introduces a one-step approach to integrate multi-enzyme activity and bone-targeting properties into a single nanostructure, providing a novel strategy to precisely halt the progression of RA.

All chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and used without further purification. Alendronate sodium (98 %) was purchased from J&K Scientific (China). Hemin chloride (98 %), urea (≥99.5 %), and FITC-phalloidin were sourced from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (China), Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. (China), and Yeasen Biotechnology (40735ES75), respectively. Hydroxyapatite (HA) was obtained from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. (China). DAPI (C1002) was purchased from Beyotime Institute of Biotechnology. Xanthine (X, ≥99 %) and FeSO4 (≥98 %) were obtained from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. (China). Xanthine oxidase (XO) was obtained from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (China).

The CDs were synthesized through a hydrothermal protocol as follows: hemin chloride and urea were combined in a 1:2 M ratio and dissolved in 40 mL of dilute NaOH solution under vigorous stirring. Alendronate sodium was subsequently introduced into the reaction system at varying molar ratios (0, 1:1, 1:2). The homogeneous solution was then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and hydrothermally treated at 150 °C for 6 h. After cooling to ambient temperature, the product was filtered through 0.22 μm polyethersulfone (PES) membranes and dialyzed (3500 Da) for 72 h to obtain purified CDs solutions. Three distinct formulations were prepared based on alendronate sodium incorporation: CDs1 (control group, without alendronate sodium), CDs2 (1:1 M ratio of hemin chloride to alendronate sodium), and CDs3 (1:2 M ratio). All synthesized CDs solutions were stored in the dark at 4 °C prior to characterization.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) were conducted using a JEM-2100F microscopy (JEOL, Japan). The functional groups of CDs were analyzed via Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Bruker TENSOR II, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was recorded with a Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250xi system with Al Kα radiation. Zeta potential measurements were performed using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90 instrument (UK). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded on a HITACHI F-7000 spectrophotometer. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were obtained using a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrometer. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra were collected on a Bruker EMXplus-6/1 system. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were acquired using a Bruker D8 ADVANCE diffractometer. Endocytosis experiment was conducted using a fluorescent inverted microscope (Nikon, Ta2-FL). A laser scanning confocal microscope (Olympus, FV10i) was employed to evaluate the ability of CDs to scavenge ROS. The targeting capability of CDs was assessed with a PerkinElmer IVIS Spectrum imaging system. High-resolution small animal micro-computed tomography (micro-CT) imaging was performed on rat hind limbs using a Bruker Daltonik GmbH SKYSCAN 1276.
Catalase (CAT)-like activity: CDs solution (0.1 mg/mL) were mixed 1 mM H2O2. Oxygen bubble generation was recorded by digital imaging.

Superoxide dismutase (SOD)-like activity: The samples were prepared as described in the following procedure and incubated together for 10 min before immediate testing. Absorption spectra were obtained under dark condition. Specifically, 20 μL of 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) (100 mM) and 180 μL of methanol were mixed, along with the xanthine (10 mM, 100 μL) and xanthine oxidase (1 U/mL, 100 μL). Finally, 180 μL of CDs (0.5 mg/mL) was added.

Hydroxyl radical (•OH) scavenging: For the scavenging of •OH, 20 μL of 5-tert-butoxycarbonyl 5-methyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (BMPO) (100 mM) and 160 μL of phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) or CDs (0.5 mg/mL) were added to 200 μL of FeSO4 solution (5 mg/mL). The mixture was then added to 20 μL of H2O2 (30 %) and stirred at room temperature for 5 min before the ESR measurements. Absorption spectra were obtained under dark condition.

Fresh heparin sodium-anticoagulated whole blood samples were collected. After centrifugation, plasma and leukocyte layers were removed. Erythrocytes were washed three times with PBS, and a 4 % erythrocyte suspension was prepared. CDs1 and CDs3 were serially diluted with normal saline to obtain final concentrations of 0.01, 0.1, and 0.5 mg/mL, respectively. The samples were then mixed with an equal volume of erythrocyte suspension to achieve a final erythrocyte concentration of 2 %. A negative control group (PBS + erythrocyte suspension) and a positive control group (1 % Triton X-100 + erythrocyte suspension) were set up. After all the groups were incubated at 37 °C for 12 h, the samples were centrifuged. The supernatants were collected, and their absorbance was measured at 540 nm. The hemolysis rate was calculated using the following formula: Hemolysis rate (%) = [(absorbance of the sample group - absorbance of the negative control group)/(absorbance of the positive control group - absorbance of the negative control group)] × 100 %.

After incubating logarithmic growth phase RAW264.7 cells with CDs (0.05 mg/mL) for 24 h, the slides were fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde for 15 min. Then wash the slides three times with phosphate buffered saline with tween 20 (PBST), 3 min each time. Add diluted phalloidin solution (5 μg/mL) and incubate at 37 °C for 1 h. Afterward, wash the slides three times with PBST, 3 min each time. Add DAPI dye and incubate in the dark for 5 min to stain the nuclei. Wash the slides four times with PBST for 5 min each time to remove excess DAPI. Finally, gently blot the slides with absorbent paper to remove excess liquid, and mount using a mounting medium containing antifade reagent. Images were captured using a fluorescence microscope. FITC-labeled phalloidin is shown in green.

RAW 264.7 cells in the logarithmic growth phase with good viability were seeded at 2 × 105 cells/well in confocal cell culture plates and incubated overnight at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 environment. The cells were treated as follows: control group, H2O2 control group, H2O2 + CDs1 (0.1 mg/mL), and H2O2 + CDs3 (0.1 mg/mL) for 24 h. Each group of RAW 264.7 cells was washed three times with PBS. 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) was diluted in serum-free culture medium at a ratio of 1:1000 to achieve a final concentration of 10 μmol/L. Diluted DCFH-DA was added to the cells and incubated in a 37 °C cell culture incubator for 20 min. The cells were then washed three times with serum-free culture medium to thoroughly remove any unincorporated DCFH-DA. Observations were performed using a confocal microscope with excitation and emission wavelengths of 488 and 525 nm, respectively.

All geometric and energy calculations were executed within the framework of the density functional theory (DFT) method implemented in the VASP code [29]. The exchange-correlation potential was described by the Generalized Gradient Approximation (GGA) with–the Perdew–Burke Ernzerhof functional (PBE) [30]. The projector augmented wave method was used to account for core-valence interactions [31,32]. The kinetic energy cutoff for plane wave expansions was set to 520 eV. The energy criterion was set to 10-5 eV in the iterative solution of the Kohn-Sham equation. All structures were relaxed until the residual forces on the atoms declined to less than 0.02 eV/Å. According to this model, the changes in Gibbs free energy (ΔG) for all enzyme-like reaction steps were defined as: ΔG = ΔE + ΔEZPE-TΔS, where the reaction energy (ΔE) can be directly obtained by analyzing the DFT total energies. The zero-point energy difference (ΔEZPE) between the products and reactants was computed from the vibrational frequencies. ΔS is the change in entropy between products and reactants at room temperature (T = 298.15 K).

Where EHA/CDs represents the energy of HA after the adsorption of CDs, EHA represents the energy of HA, and ECDs represents the energy of CDs.

Healthy SPF-grade male SD rats (6-8 weeks old) were purchased, and complete Freund's adjuvant was thoroughly emulsified with bovine type II collagen using a homogenizer. The initial immunization was performed via five subcutaneous injections on the backs of the rats. One week later, a booster injection was administered, followed by emulsification of bovine type II collagen with incomplete Freund's adjuvant using the same method. RA onset was observed approximately one week after the second injection. CIA model rats with similar arthritis indices were divided into three groups: one positive control group, one group treated with CDs1, and one group treated with CDs3. All dosages were 5 mg/kg. During the experiment, changes in the rat body weight, arthritis index scores, and ankle joint thickness were recorded. At the end of the experiment, the rats were anesthetized and euthanized, and the major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys) along with the left and right ankle joints (hind legs) were collected. The organs were fixed and stained using hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) to evaluate the potential toxicity of the CDs. Ankle joints were fixed, decalcified, dehydrated, and embedded in paraffin for H&E staining, allowing for observation of synovial tissue hyperplasia, angiogenesis, inflammatory cell infiltration, and bone destruction. Micro-CT technology was utilized to assess bone destruction in each group of rats.

Specifically, 5 μL of 5 mg/mL Cy5 DMSO solution was added to 1 mg/mL CDs PBS solution, and the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 2 h. The reaction mixture was then subjected to dialysis purification for 48 h to remove unreacted Cy5 molecules. For HA binding analysis, CDs solutions with gradient concentrations (0, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5 mg/mL) were incubated with 10 mg of HA particles for 2 h under physiological conditions. After centrifugation, HA-bound CDs complexes were collected and resuspended in PBS. The binding affinity between CDs and HA was qualitatively assessed through real-time fluorescence imaging using an in vivo small animal imaging system.

After the experimental mice were euthanized with isoflurane, both femurs were immediately dissected and isolated, and the attached soft tissues were removed. The intact femurs were respectively immersed in the solutions of Cy5-fluorescently labeled CDs1 and CDs3 (1 mg/mL in PBS, pH 7.4) and co-incubated at 37 °C in the dark for 12 h. After incubation, the bone tissues were washed three times (10 min each time) on a shaker with pre-cooled PBS (4 °C) to remove unbound CDs. Finally, fluorescent images of the femurs were collected using an IVIS® Spectrum In Vivo Imaging System (PerkinElmer, USA) under the Cy5 channel (Ex/Em = 640/680 nm).

To evaluate the biodistribution and disease site accumulation, Cy5-labeled CDs were administered intravenously to CIA model rats. Longitudinal fluorescence imaging was performed at predetermined time intervals (1, 4, 8, 12, 16, and 24 h post-injection) using the IVIS® spectrum imaging system to monitor the spatiotemporal distribution patterns.

Data are expressed as the mean ± standard deviation (SD, n = 3). Between-group differences were assessed via an unpaired t-test (two groups) or Tukey's ANOVA (multiple groups) using GraphPad Prism 9.5. Statistical significance was set at p < 0.05.

Three nitrogen/phosphorus-containing CDs were synthesized via a one-pot method using hemin chloride, alendronate, and urea in different molar ratios and labeled as CDs1, CDs2, and CDs3, corresponding to increasing alendronate content. They exhibited typical zero-dimensional structures, with particle sizes of 3.57 ± 0.02 nm, 4.17 ± 0.16 nm, and 3.54 ± 0.05 nm, respectively (Fig. 1 a–c). Notably, CDs2 displayed a larger particle size compared to the other two groups and exhibited a "worm-like" morphology. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was employed to observe the crystalline structure, as illustrated in the insets of Fig. 1a–c. The interlayer spacing for all CDs was 0.19 nm, corresponding to the (102) plane of graphite [33]. The Zeta potential analysis revealed that CDs exhibited a negative charge, with the potential becoming increasingly negative as the molar ratio of alendronate increased. (Fig. 1d). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was utilized to investigate the functional groups (Fig. 1e). The peak at 3438 cm−1 corresponded to O-H stretching vibrations [34,35], while the peak at 3205 cm−1 was attributed to N-H stretching vibrations [36]. CDs3 exhibited distinct characteristic peaks at 1043 and 1137 cm−1, corresponding to P-OH and P=O [37], respectively, indicating a significant presence of phosphate groups on its surface. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns (Fig. S1) revealed a broad and weak diffraction peak centered at 21.5°, corresponding to a d-spacing of 4.1 Å, suggesting a disruption of the π-π stacking of sp2 domains in the core [38]. Raman spectroscopy analysis (Fig. S2) showed that the peaks at 1385 cm−1 and 1569 cm−1 corresponded to the sp3 (D band) and sp2 (G band) domains, respectively. The ID/IG ratio represents the ratio of disordered domains to graphite domains. The ID/IG values for CDs1, CDs, and CDs3 are 0.87, 0.85, and 0.79, respectively, indicating that the degree of graphitization gradually increases. These data confirm that phosphorus doping enhances the structural ordering of CDs.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) provided a comprehensive analysis of the chemical composition of the CDs, confirming the presence of C, O, N, and P (Fig. 1f). The elemental compositions of the three CDs are listed in Table S1. The phosphorus contents in CDs1, CDs2, and CDs3 gradually increased, reaching 0 %, 1.13 %, and 3.16 %, respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 1g, Fig. S3) indicated that the binding energies, in increasing order, corresponded to C=C/C-C (283.9 eV), C-C/C-N/C-P (284.7 eV), C-O/C=N/C=P (285.6 eV), and C=O/O-C=O (288.6 eV) [39,40]. The carbon composition transitions from C=C/C-C in CDs1 to C=N/C=P in CDs3. The O1s spectrum revealed two peaks corresponding to C-O/O-(N/P) (530.9 eV) and C=O/P=O (532.1 eV) (Fig. 1h, Fig. S3) [41]. The intensity of the first peak decreased from CDs1 to CDs3, while the intensity of the second peak increased. The N1s spectrum shows the presence of three distinct nitrogen functional groups (Fig. S3): pyridinic nitrogen (398.3 eV), graphitic nitrogen (399.7 eV), and an amine peak (401.2 eV) [42]. The amino group contents on the surfaces of CDs1, CDs2, and CDs3 increased sequentially to 8.6 %, 18.7 %, and 33.8 %, respectively, which was attributed to the incorporation of bisphosphonates during the synthesis of CDs2 and CDs3. The Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. S3) displayed two peaks at 710.4 and 712.8 eV for Fe 2p3/2, and two peaks at 724.2 and 728.3 eV for Fe 2p1/2, indicating the presence of both Fe2+ and Fe3+ species [43]. The P2p spectrum (Fig. 1i) of the CDs3 revealed peaks at 132.5 and 133.1 eV corresponding to P-O and P-C bonds [44]. These results demonstrate that the CDs3 effectively retained the functional groups of the precursors, providing abundant catalytic sites for their multi-enzyme activity. Fe content was quantitatively analyzed by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), revealing concentrations of 257.4 ± 2.6, 229.8 ± 3.7, and 48.9 ± 0.2 mg/L for CDs1, CDs2, and CDs3, respectively. The trend of variation is consistent with that of the XPS results. Notably, the introduction of alendronate sodium in CDs3 led to increased O and P contents, accompanied by reduced C and Fe levels. Compared to CDs1, CDs3 incorporates a significant number of phosphorus-containing functional groups, establishing a foundation for its bone-targeting capabilities.

As shown in Fig. S4, all three CDs exhibited broad absorption capabilities across the UV to red-light spectrum. Notably, a significant ultraviolet absorption peak between 300 and 400 nm was attributed to the π-π∗ transitions of C=C and C=N. Additionally, a broad absorption in the range of 400–600 nm corresponded to n-π∗ transitions related to C=N or C=O [45]. A plot of CDs concentration against absorbance at 300-400 nm revealed a good linear relationship for CDs1 and CDs3 within the concentration range of 0.01–2 mg/mL, whereas CDs2 showed a linear relationship between 0.01 and 1 mg/mL. From the fluorescence emission spectra in Fig. S5, it is evident that CDs1, CDs2, and CDs3 exhibited minimal emission behavior.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the enzyme-like activity of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: We further investigated the ability of CDs to scavenge ROS. DMPO was utilized as an ESR trapping agent to capture the •OH radicals generated by the Fenton reaction. The ESR spectra of the DMPO/•OH adducts are presented in Fig. 2 a. The signal intensities of DMPO/•OH for CDs1, CDs2, and CDs3 progressively decreased, with CDs3 exhibiting superior •OH scavenging abilities. The O2 •- produced from the reaction between xanthine and xanthine oxidase can be captured by DMPO. The ESR signal of DMPO/•OOH adduct significantly diminished in the presence of CDs, with CDs3 demonstrating enhanced O2 •- scavenging capacity (Fig. 2b). H2O2, a byproduct of O2 •- dismutation, is highly stable and represents the most abundant intracellular ROS, making its removal crucial for protecting organisms from oxidative damage [46]. The addition of CDs to H2O2 solution rapidly generated a large number of O2 bubbles, indicating CAT-like activity of CDs (Fig. 2c). Furthermore, the number of bubbles increased with increasing CDs concentration (Fig. S6). The catalytic decomposition of H2O2 by CDs was further evaluated by measuring oxygen generation. As shown in Fig. S7, under identical conditions, the oxygen production capacity followed the order CDs3 > CDs2 > CDs1, with maximum concentrations of 20, 28, and 35 mg/L achieved at 45 s. This demonstrates that the CAT-like enzyme activity of CDs3 is superior. Previous studies confirmed that iron porphyrin-derived CDs host atomically dispersed Fe-N4 sites, whereas iron-free counterparts lack catalytic activity, underscoring iron's indispensability as an active center [47]. In this study, phosphorus doping further optimized the electronic configuration of CDs by introducing additional catalytic sites, which synergistically enhanced the activity in conjunction with the iron centers.

To elucidate the structure-activity relationship of CDs enzymatic activity, density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed to systematically analyze the adsorption-activation-dissociation mechanism of H2O2 at different doping sites, revealing the molecular basis for the enhancement of catalytic activity through phosphorus doping. Fig. 2d illustrates the structural changes that occur during the catalytic reaction of H2O2 with CDs3. As shown in Fig. 2e, the first half of the reaction includes the adsorption and activation of H2O2, followed by uniform dissociation, forming a surface reactive OH∗ group attached to the active site. When H2O2 adsorbs on the Fe metal site of CDs1, the energy is relatively high, which is unfavorable for H2O2 activation and dissociation into OH∗. In contrast, H2O2 is more readily adsorbed and dissociated at lower-energy CDs2 and CDs3 sites, indicating that these sites facilitate the adsorption, activation, and dissociation of H2O2 into OH∗. In the latter half of the process, the OH∗ group bound to the surface undergoes protonation to generate O∗, and the adjacent 2O∗ groups readily recombine via a significant endothermic process to form an oxygen bridge structure (O2∗), which then desorbs as O2. In conclusion, the introduction of higher amounts of phosphates and phosphorus doping enhances the catalytic activity of the enzyme [48].

The peroxidase (POD)-like catalytic reactions on the three types of surface models were simulated, which resembled the first half of the CAT-like enzyme catalytic reactions. Notably, the OH group could dissociate the second •OH radical without forming another intermediate. The key intermediate structures and free-energy distributions during the reaction are shown in Fig. 2f. Clearly, the dissociation of H2O2 was the rate-determining step for the entire reaction. When H2O2 is adsorbed on the CDs1 site, the adsorption energy is relatively high, which is unfavorable for H2O2 activation and dissociation into OH∗. In contrast, H2O2 is more easily adsorbed and dissociated at the lower-energy CDs2 and CDs3 sites, indicating that these sites aid in the adsorption, activation, and dissociation of H2O2 into OH∗. These results suggest that the introduction of higher amounts of phosphates improves enzymatic catalytic activity. The lower adsorption energies of H2O2 on CDs2 and CDs3 indicate that these sites are involved in the adsorption, activation, and dissociation of H2O2 into OH∗.

The phosphorus atom possesses lone pair electrons, which, upon doping, act as electron donors to modulate the electron cloud distribution of CDs, thereby enhancing their reducing capacity and enabling more efficient scavenging of ROS [49]. Moreover, phosphorus doping introduces phosphorus-containing functional groups, which provide additional catalytic active sites to promote electron transfer reactions during ROS elimination. For instance, Lin et al. [50] synthesized a phosphorus-doped single-atom iron-based nanowire with high POD-like activity and explored the regulatory mechanism of phosphorus to enhance POD-like activity. With the introduction of phosphorus atoms, the variation trends of the Löwdin charges of Fe, N, and P demonstrated that doping with a small amount of phosphorus could endow iron atoms with higher catalytic activity.

Subsequently, the influence of different phosphorus elements in CDs on the catalytic performance of O2 •- was analyzed. It has been demonstrated that in aqueous solutions, O2 •- readily abstracts protons from water, resulting in the formation of •OOH. Consequently, in DFT simulations, •OOH was employed as a surrogate for O2 •- [51]. The activity of H2O2 was dependent on the binding strength of the OOH∗ intermediate (Fig. 2g). Thus, the rate-determining step is the formation of OOH∗. After anchoring to the metal site, the rate-determining step shifted to the desorption of OOH∗. The free energies of CDs1, CDs2, and CDs3 are 2.42, 2.07, and 1.84 V, respectively. Therefore, due to the weaker binding of OOH∗, the activity of CDs3 is higher than that of CDs1 and CDs2 sites [52]. These results confirm that the introduction of electronegative secondary heteroatoms (such as phosphorus) into nitrogen-doped carbon frameworks can regulate the synergistic structural evolution of the central metal atom, thereby effectively enhancing catalytic activity [53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antioxidant activity of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the cytotoxicity of CDs, RAW264.7 cells were co-cultured with different concentrations of CDs for 24 h. The CCK-8 assay showed that the three types of CDs exhibited good biocompatibility, maintaining a proliferation rate exceeding 90 % at a concentration of 0.5 mg/mL (Fig. S8). The hemolysis assay demonstrated that CDs, when co-incubated with red blood cells at concentrations of 0.01, 0.1, and 0.5 mg/mL for 12 h, exhibited hemolysis rates of less than 10 %, with no significant difference compared to the PBS negative control group (NC), indicating good blood compatibility. In contrast, the hemolysis rate of the positive control group (PC) was greater than 96 % (Fig. 3 a). Subsequently, we investigated the endocytic behavior of RAW264.7 cells toward CDs. The nuclei were stained with DAPI (blue), the F-actin cytoskeleton was visualized using phalloidin (green), and CDs were labeled with Cy5 (red). After co-incubation of the cells with CDs for 24 h and subsequent fixation, Cy5 red fluorescence was primarily localized in the cytoplasm rather than in the nucleus (Fig. 3b). This characteristic avoids the risk of direct contact between nanomaterials and genetic material, thereby significantly reducing potential DNA damage or genotoxicity. In contrast, CDs can regulate abnormal activation of macrophages by scavenging various ROS within cells. Particularly in disease lesions, this ROS scavenging ability can intervene in the excessive polarization of M1 pro-inflammatory macrophages, breaking the vicious cycle of inflammation and ultimately achieving therapeutic effects to delay or inhibit disease progression [54,55].

Given that CDs3 exhibited superior ROS-scavenging capability, we selected CDs1 as the blank control and CDs3 as the experimental group for subsequent studies simulating a high oxidative stress microenvironment with H2O2. The ROS-specific probe DCFH-DA was used to assess intracellular ROS levels. As illustrated in Fig. 3c, under H2O2 stimulation, the cell volume of RAW264.7 cells increased compared to the control group, a phenomenon associated with cell edema induced by oxidative stress (including mitochondrial swelling and endoplasmic reticulum expansion). Pre-treatment with CDs significantly inhibited the H2O2-induced increase in cell volume. Furthermore, the green fluorescence intensity in the CDs1 and CDs3 groups decreased. We further performed quantitative analysis of the fluorescence signals using ImageJ software and found that the fluorescence area percentages for the four groups were 0.095 %, 2.935 %, 1.181 %, and 0.582 %, respectively. The results indicate that, compared to CDs1, CDs3 exhibits significantly lower fluorescence intensity, suggesting a superior intracellular ROS scavenging capacity of CDs3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Verification of the bone-targeting ability of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To elucidate the binding mechanism between CDs and bone, the binding energies of CDs and hydroxyapatite (HA, a major bone component) were calculated. The results indicated that an increase in the number of phosphate groups on the surface of CDs led to a lower binding energy with HA (Fig. 4 a). The Ca2+ ions in HA can chelate with the phosphate groups of the "P-C-P" structure, forming a highly stable chelation ring [56–58]. Therefore, CDs3 interacts more readily with bone tissue. The binding affinity of CDs to HA was assessed in vitro. The reaction between the NHS ester group on Cy5 and amine groups on the surface of CDs was employed to conjugate Cy5 to the CDs. Subsequently, dialysis was performed to remove any unbound dye. CDs1, CDs2, and CDs3 were co-incubated with HA at varying concentrations. The degree of binding was quantified by measuring fluorescence intensity. As illustrated in Fig. 4b and S9, the binding capacities of all three CDs to HA increased in proportion to CDs concentration. However, no significant differences were observed at low concentrations. At concentrations of 0.1, 0.2, and 0.5 mg/mL, the fluorescence intensity followed the order CDs3 > CDs2 > CDs1. Notably, while the fluorescence intensities of all three CDs increased with concentration, this relationship did not exhibit a linear concentration-dependent trend but rather reached gradual saturation at higher concentrations. It is evident that CDs3 interacts more readily with HA than with CDs1 and CDs2 at equivalent concentrations (Fig. 4c). To validate the stability of the dye conjugation and exclude potential dye detachment, fluorescence intensity-matched CDs samples were dialyzed in PBS (pH 7.4) at 37 °C for 24 h. During dialysis, 10 μL aliquots were collected at 2, 6, 12, 18, and 24 h intervals for fluorescence quantification using a NanoDrop™ spectrophotometer. As demonstrated in Fig. S10, the fluorescence intensity remained statistically unchanged over the 24-h period (p > 0.05, one-way ANOVA), confirming the robust covalent linkage. These results substantiate the reliability of dye-labeled CDs for in vivo biodistribution assessments.

In vivo fluorescence imaging was performed using Cy5-labeled CDs (devoid of HA) to investigate the distribution of CDs1 and CDs3 in the joints of CIA rats at various time points (Fig. 5 a). The results were analyzed by quantifying the integrated fluorescence intensity across the four limbs (Fig. 5b). The data revealed that CDs1 exhibited minimal accumulation in the affected joints, with the majority of the intensity attributed to background noise. Notably, the accumulation intensity of CDs3 at joint sites was significantly higher than that of CDs1. The fluorescence intensity of both CDs1 and CDs3 gradually decreased over time at 8 h post-administration. This observation may be due to the abundant phosphate groups on the surface of CDs3, which strongly bind to calcium ions in the bone tissue, thereby reducing the metabolic rate of CDs3 at the joint site and facilitating its continuous accumulation in the affected areas. Subsequently, we co-incubated the fluorescent dyes labeled CDs1 and CDs3 with mouse femurs to assess the binding ability of CDs to bone tissue. As shown in Fig. 5c and d, the fluorescence intensity of CDs3 was higher than that of CDs1 (∗∗p = 0.003), indicating that CDs3 has greater binding ability to bone tissue, providing evidence for further in vivo imaging. We further performed cardiac perfusion and limb collection 8 h after the injection of CDs. Imaging analysis revealed that the fluorescence intensity of the limbs corresponding to CDs3 was significantly higher than that of CDs1 (Fig. 5e and f), consistent with the in vivo imaging data. These results highlight the potential of CDs3 for targeted therapy of bone-related diseases, such as RA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo therapeutic efficacy evaluation of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Based on previous material and cell experiments, it is evident that CDs1 exhibits the weakest performance in terms of bone targeting and free radical scavenging ability, while CDs3 demonstrates the strongest performance. Therefore, in subsequent animal experiments, only CDs1 and CDs3 were selected for investigation to observe the differences in their in vivo therapeutic effects. The therapeutic effects were characterized by morphological observations, including body weight, clinical scores, and paw thickness. Following secondary immunization, symptoms of RA, such as joint redness and swelling, were progressively observed. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the negative control (NC) group exhibited the greatest increase in body weight, while the CIA model group experienced a reduction in weight. Rats in the treatment groups showed a slight increase in body weight. The arthritis index (AI) scores presented in Fig. 6b indicate that the clinical scores for the CIA model group were significantly higher than those of the control group, confirming the successful establishment of the RA model. The AI scores of the CIA model group showed minimal variation, with a slight decrease due to the progression of the CIA model from the inflammatory stage to cartilage and bone destruction, leading to a reduction in inflammation-related symptoms. In contrast, both treatment groups displayed a significant decrease in AI scores post-treatment, with the CDs3 treatment group demonstrating a more rapid decline. This difference is further illustrated by the changes in ankle joint diameter, as shown in Fig. 6c. Collectively, these results suggest that the tail vein injection of CDs3 leads to a better therapeutic effect.

Additionally, we performed a histological assessment of the ankle joints. After treatment, the hind limb ankle joints were fixed and decalcified, followed by H&E staining of the decalcified bone tissue to analyze the pathological damage (Fig. 6d). The results indicated significant inflammatory cell infiltration, synovial hyperplasia, cartilage damage, and bone destruction were observed in the CIA group. In contrast, the CDs3 group exhibited the mildest pathological changes, with a notable reduction in inflammatory cells, a relatively intact joint structure, and minimal cellular exudate in the joint cavity. Micro-CT analysis of the joints in the CIA group displayed rough bone surfaces (Fig. 6e), indicative of severe bone erosion and decreased bone density. Upon magnification of the ankle joint and phalanges (indicated by the red box), it was observed that the bone surfaces in the CDs group appeared smoother, with the CDs3 treatment group showing levels closer to those of the normal rats. After 21 days of treatment, no significant tissue damage or inflammatory lesions were observed in any major organ, including the heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys (Fig. S11). Therefore, we concluded that within a certain range of dosage concentrations, CDs exhibited good biocompatibility.

In conclusion, RA management poses formidable challenges, particularly in addressing the oxidative stress microenvironment. To address this issue, we developed biocompatible multi-enzyme activity CDs with a porphyrin iron core and rich heteroatoms as defect regulators. These phosphorus-rich CDs not only significantly enhance the scavenging efficiency of various ROS but also exhibit a high affinity for bone tissue. Consequently, these CDs could efficiently eliminate excess intracellular ROS. The abundant phosphate groups on the surface provide targeted specificity for bone tissue. Owing to the combined effects of high multi-enzyme activity and targeting capability, CDs can effectively alleviate arthritis symptoms, reduce local inflammatory responses, and inhibit cartilage and bone destruction. Therefore, this study integrates multiple catalytic sites and targeting groups into a single nanostructure, offering a novel and effective strategy for the precise treatment of oxidative stress-related bone diseases.
